name: One-and-a-Half Asses
content: The pledge master hands you a book. It is empty—it is a notebook, no longer on the sides than a Post-It and a half. Inside are fields to fill. The fields, however, are penned individually, and with beauty and, most importantly, love.
The act of trivial artistry is not lost. Lucky cups of hot chocolate are donned with hearts from a bisexual Barista; the crying boy on the airplane gets a cute note from cabin crew.

At the start of each semester, Resident Advisors are given extensive sets of tasks to perform before dorms are ready for move-in. One such task is the adornment of each door with nametags—or, rather, pieces of paper that will end up becoming nametags. There is a corporate-souled template pre-printed on high-quality sheets, and there is tape; there is not much else to the task. Yet, many-a-floors are home to doors with more humanity than that: chart paper is selected, black markers exhausted, and in the confines of a still-nondescript abode there is a Resident Advisor writing fifty versions of “My Name is…”. This is materially unrewarded work. To call it work may be a sort of injustice to the artisan, but effort is nevertheless expended on the non-requisite.

Romanticists argue that art looks for outlets; nametag curation becomes a focality for the right side of the brain and we insert a little bit of ourselves wherever we can. The impersonal then feels no longer so distant for the customer—there is a human being behind this nametag, and I think that’s ever-so-slightly more beautiful than a more perfectly computed rendering.
